coredata.dc:title: Hydroxyapatite supported N-doped carbon quantum dots for visible-light photocatalysis
coredata.dc:creator: Chang, Q.
Li, K.K.
Hu, S.L.
Dong, Y.G.
Yang, J.L.
coredata.dc:description: Development of effective and green photocatalysts that are sensitive to visible light is highly crucial. The purpose of this investigation was to develop a new hydroxyapatite (HA) supported N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) photocatalyst via a facile chemical strategy. The FTIR and TEM results confirmed that N-CQDs were successfully coupled with HA. The formed HA/N-CQDs composite exhibited a pronounced photocatalytic performance upon the degradation of methylene blue (MB) under visible-light irradiation. The degradation rate of MB by the composite was almost two times that of N-CQDs. The mechanisms for the improvement of the photocatalytic performance of the composites were discussed on the basis of the results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hydroxyapatite
N-doped carbon quantum dots
Nanocomposites
Microstructure
Photocatalysis

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A) XRD patterns of HA, HA/N-CQDs (S2), JCPDS (009-0432) of HA, and JCPDS (003-0401) of graphite; B) FTIR spectra of HA, N-CQDs and HA/N-CQDs (S2).
A) UV/vis absorption spectra of HA, N-CQDs and HA/N-CQDs (S2); B) The visible-light photodegradation (>420nm) of methylene blue by HA, N-CQDs and HA/N-CQDs composites.

A) Schematic model for the electron and hole movements in the HA/N-CQDs composites; B) PL spectra of TAOH formed by the reaction of TA with ∙ OH radicals generated by HA, N-CQDs and HA/N-CQDs (S2), under visible-light irradiation for 30min.. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: Hydroxyapatite supported N-doped carbon quantum dots for visible-light photocatalysis

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, North University of China, Xueyuan Road 3, Taiyuan 030051, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering, North University of China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of New Ceramics and Fine Processing, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of New Ceramics and Fine Processing, Tsinghua University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Development of effective and green photocatalysts that are sensitive to visible light is highly crucial. The purpose of this investigation was to develop a new hydroxyapatite (HA) supported N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) photocatalyst via a facile chemical strategy. The FTIR and TEM results confirmed that N-CQDs were successfully coupled with HA. The formed HA/N-CQDs composite exhibited a pronounced photocatalytic performance upon the degradation of methylene blue (MB) under visible-light irradiation. The degradation rate of MB by the composite was almost two times that of N-CQDs. The mechanisms for the improvement of the photocatalytic performance of the composites were discussed on the basis of the results.

ce:section.ce:para: The important role of heterogeneous photocatalysis in resolving the environmental pollution has been highlighted in many reports [1]. Despite the success of existing heterogeneous photocatalysis, the variety of visible-light photocatalysts is still very limited. Therefore, it is necessary to develop new and efficient photocatalytic materials. Hydroxyapatite (HA, Ca10(PO4)6(OH)2), due to its excellent biocompatibility and physical chemistry stability as well as non-toxicity, is a widely used material in the application of biomaterials [2], adsorbents [3], and ion exchangers [4]. Growing evidence has also indicated the potential usefulness of hydroxyapatite as green photocatalysts [5] or photocatalyst supports [6]. Given its versatility in the design of the nanocomposites [7,8], more research is deserved on the development of HA for potential use as photocatalysts or photocatalyst supports.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) is a new class of nanomaterials that possess excellent electronic and physicochemical characteristics, such as broadband optical absorption, energy exchange capacity, strong photoluminescence (PL) emission, chemical stability, and non-toxicity [9,10]. These unique properties of CQDs make them to be a legitimate competitor to the conventional photocatalyst TiO2 [11–13]. More recently, N-doped CQDs (N-CQDs) have been developed and showed much better performance than CQDs [14,15].

Interestingly, Guo et al. [16] reported that amine-activated CdSe dots were able to conjugate with HA by the covalent reaction between amine and hydroxyl. Thus, provided combining HA and N-doped CQDs, the similar reaction between amine on the surface of N-CQDs and hydroxyl on the surface of HA would happen. The purpose of this investigation was, therefore, to synthesize hydroxyapatite supported N-CQDs composites in an attempt to develop a novel and green photocatalyst with good photocatalytic performance, and identify the underlying photocatalytic mechanisms.

HA nanopowders were synthesized through a hydrothermal method by reacting CaCl2 with Na2HPO4·12H2O. The calcium solution and phosphate solution were prepared separately. The phosphate solution at a Ca/P molar ratio of 1.67 was added drop-wise to the calcium solution at room temperature. The pH value of the reacting solution was kept to 11 by adding ammonia. The mixture was then transferred to the Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 150°C for 15h. After the autoclave cooled to room temperature, the precipitate was separated by centrifugation and washed with distilled water. The resulting precipitate was dried at 80°C. The average size of the obtained HA powders was less than 100nm.
N-CQDs were fabricated via the procedure reported previously [11].

0.3g obtained HA powders were well dispersed in 5mL, 10mL, and 20mL N-CQDs aqueous solution, respectively. After stirring for 30min, the three mixed solutions were respectively transferred to the Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 140°C for 4h. After the autoclave cooled to room temperature, the HA/N-CQDs precipitates were separated by centrifugation and washed with distilled water, then dried at 50°C for 12h. The obtained HA/N-CQDs samples were marked as S1, S2 and S3.

The phase of the synthesized samples was analyzed via X-ray diffraction (XRD) using CuKα radiation at 40kV and 30mA. Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) were recorded with a Thermo Nicolet 360 FTIR spectrometer. The morphology was investigated by a Tecnai G2 F20 transmission electron microscopy (TEM). UV/vis absorption spectra were measured using a Shimadzu UV-2550 spectrophotometer in the wavelength range of 200–1000nm.

The photocatalytic activity was evaluated from the photodegradation of aqueous methylene blue (MB) solution at room temperature. 40mg samples were dispersed in 40mL MB solution (20mg/L) in 50mL beaker. Prior to irradiation, the solution was stirred for 1h in dark to ensure the establishment of an adsorption-desorption equilibrium. A 300W Xe arc lamp with a cutoff filter (λ<420nm) was used as a visible light source. After visible-light irradiation for some time, the reaction solution was centrifuged for measurement the concentration of MB.

Fig. 1 shows the XRD patterns and FTIR spectra of the synthesized samples. The obtained HA exhibited a typical XRD pattern of HA (JCPDS, 009-0432), and HA/N-CQDs composite exhibited similar pattern with HA (Fig. 1A). Form FTIR spectra (Fig. 1B), it was observed that HA/N-CQDs composite (S2) showed the characteristic peaks of C–O, C–N, and C=NH– (or –C≡N) at 1375, 1630, and 2300cm−1, which were the typical characteristic absorption of N-CQDs. Additionally, it was found that the sharp peak at 3570cm−1 due to –OH stretching vibration disappeared in the HA/N-CQDs composite, suggesting that –OH on the surface of HA had reacted with –NH2 on the surface of N-CQDs.

Fig. 2 shows the morphology of the HA/N-CQDs composite (S2). It can be seen that N-CQDs were randomly coupled on the HA surface. In addition, the interplanar spacing of the CQDs (0.32nm) and HA (0.28nm) were observed, which corresponded to (002) of CQDs and (211) of HA, respectively. All the above results strongly indicated that the N-CQDs were successfully coupled with HA.

Fig. 3A shows the UV/vis absorption spectra of HA, N-CQDs and HA/N-CQDs composites. It can be seen that HA has no absorption above its fundamental absorption sharp edge rising at about 300nm due to its intrinsic wide band gap [5]. For N-CQDs, an apparent absorption peak at 270nm was observed, which was caused by the transition from n to π* of C=O [11]. A significant result observed in this study was that the absorption edges of HA/N-CQDs shifted to red and exhibited a wider absorption, which implied that the composites may have higher photocatalytic activity. The photocatalytic performance of the synthesized samples was shown in Fig. 3B. HA exhibited little photocatalytic ability under visible irradiation. N-CQDs showed efficient photocatalytic performance and 50% of MB was decomposed after 50min irradiation. For the HA/N-CQDs composites, it is seen that with increasing amount of N-CQDs, the photocatalytic ability increased first and then decreased. S2 exhibited the highest photocatalytic activity – the degradation efficiency of MB reached the maximum value of 90%, which was almost two times that of N-CQDs. This strongly suggested that HA/N-CQDs can be used as a new and effective visible-light responsive photocatalyst.

The excellent photocatalytic performance of the new HA/N-CQDs composite was related to two potential reasons. First, the conjugation between N-CQDs and HA due to the covalent reaction [16] could inhibit the aggregation of N-CQDs so as to reduce the chances of recombination of photoexcited electron-hole pairs. However, redundant N-CQDs could produce aggregation that makes the recombination of electron-hole pairs inevitably happen. Therefore, further increase of the content of N-CQDs resulted in a decrease of the photocatalytic activity of S3 (Fig. 3B). Second, suggested by Funakoshiw et al. [8], the storages of electrons on the HA crystals would be beneficial for improving the photocatalytic ability. As holes and electrons were generated by the photocatalytic reactions, electrons moved from the N-CQDs photocatalytic surfaces to the HA crystals so as to restrain the recombination between holes and electrons. These photoexcited electrons were then captured by oxygen to form the oxygen radicals (
∙
O 2 −
), giving rise to a higher photocatalytic performance. The schematic drawing of movements of holes and electrons between HA and N-CQDs are proposed in Fig. 4A. The amounts of ∙ OH radicals were estimated using a method described previously [17], since ∙ OH could react with terephthalic acid (TA) in basic solution to generate 2-hydroxyterephthalic acid (TAOH), which emitted a unique fluorescence signal with its peak centered at 426nm. As shown in Fig. 4B, stronger fluorescent intensity from the suspension solution of S2 containing TA was obtained in compared to N-CQDs and HA, suggesting that the composite acted as an effective photocatalyst. However, owing to the so-far limited investigations on the new HA/N-CQDs composite photocatalyst, further studies on the structure and photocatalytic mechanism are needed so as to better understand the properties of the new composites.

In this study a new HA/N-CQDs photocatalyst has been successfully achieved. N-CQDs were well conjugated on the HA surfaces. The HA/N-CQDs composite showed an increase of photocatalytic performance under visible-light irradiation by 80% in comparison to N-CQDs. The low-cost and green HA/N-CQDs photocatalyst, which combined the attractive features of HA and the excellent photochemical properties of N-CQDs, is a promising candidate for the removal of hazardous organic materials.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank the financial supports by National Natural Science Foundation of China (51172214, 51272301 and 51502270) and the Natural Science Foundation of North University of China.
